tree.title: What should I listen to right now?
tree.description: A deeply personal music recommendation tree.

branch.content: What are you doing right now?
branch.fork: Moving — workout or on the road
With people — social situation
Alone with headphones
Working or concentrating
I want to explore and discover something

branch.content: Workout or driving?
branch.fork: Workout
Driving

branch.content: What do you need from the music?
branch.fork: Aggression — push me harder
Focus — lock me in, zone everything out
Groove — keep me moving without forcing it

branch.content: Short intense session or a long grind?
branch.fork: Short — under 40 minutes
Long — over an hour

branch.content: Hit hard immediately or build into it?
branch.fork: Hit hard straight away — no warm-up
Build alongside me

branch.content: Noisia FabricLive 40. Opens without ceremony — immediate forward pressure, physically heavy in headphones. No easing in. If you need a room to disappear and a reason to keep going, this is it.
Dj St!cky Freeparty Filth 5.0. Starts rough and escalates with you. Bassline and jackin house that gets harder as the session does. Calibrated for sessions that build intensity over time.
Do you need variety to stay engaged, or does consistency help you lock in?
branch.fork: Variety — I lose focus if nothing changes
Consistency — the same energy is an anchor

branch.content: Dj St!cky Freeparty Filth 9.0. The most varied in the series — bassline, jackin, house, and garage. Enough shifts in tone to keep you from checking out across a long session without ever losing the thread of aggression.

Ed Rush and Optical — Virus Vaults Mix. Consistent neurofunk pressure across the full runtime. Nothing invites your attention to drift. Exactly the same weight from start to finish — an anchor you can set your pace against.
Do you know the scene well, or do you want something you can trust without thinking about it?
branch.fork: I know it — give me something precise
Just give me something reliable I can put on and forget

branch.content: Which end of DnB's technical spectrum?
branch.fork: Older techstep — harsh, mechanical, clinical
Modern neurofunk — refined, surgical, intricate

branch.content: Ed Rush and Optical — Wormhole. Late 90s techstep at its harshest. The production sounds deliberately cold and industrial. This is the root system of everything that followed in neurofunk — and in some ways it has never been bettered.

Noisia — FabricLive 40. The apex of modern neurofunk craft. Technically flawless, physically demanding. Put it loud enough to feel the bass and it is hard to be mentally anywhere else. The production alone is worth the listen.
Heavy or atmospheric?
branch.fork: Heavy — I want weight behind it
Atmospheric — I want to disappear into it

branch.content: Virus Vaults Mix. Heavy DnB in mix format — you do not need to know the tracks. Flows as one piece, consistent weight throughout. Set it going and let the session happen inside it.

FabricLive 46 LTJ Bukem. Atmospheric DnB designed to absorb you. The BPM keeps you moving but your mind can float completely free. Unusual for a workout but very effective for a long zone-out session where the body does the work and the mind goes elsewhere.
Electronic groove or hip hop groove?
branch.fork: Electronic — DnB or breaks
Hip hop swing

branch.content: Rolling and half-step, or more jump and bounce?
branch.fork: Rolling half-step — meditative but moving
Jump and bounce — physical energy

branch.content: Have you heard the Seba mixes before?
branch.fork: Yes, I know them well
No, or only vaguely

branch.content: Try the OPTIK mix for something slightly more minimal, or FabricLive 50 Autonomic for the broader half-step sound. Both sustain the pace without demanding effort — the rhythm does the work so you do not have to.

Seba 70 minute mix is the best entry point. Autonomic-era DnB at its most effortless — locks you into a pace without aggression. Good for workout sessions where you need to find your rhythm before you find your effort.

DJ Marky FabricLive 55 or DJ Aphrodite melodic. Brazilian funk in the DnB groove with genuine bounce — hard to stay still, harder to slow down. The kind of music that makes the workout feel shorter than it is.
Familiar tracks you can ride, or something to discover?
branch.fork: Familiar — energy I already know and trust
Something to discover while I work out

branch.content: Which era of your hip hop knowledge?
branch.fork: Beastie Boys energy — chaotic and physical
De La Soul groove — playful and rhythmic

branch.content: Licensed to Ill for pure momentum, Paul's Boutique if you want density to lose yourself in. Licensed to Ill has the more immediate physical energy — Paul's Boutique rewards the parts of a workout where your mind starts to wander.

3 Feet High and Rising. Playful, groove-heavy, and dense with detail. You will hear something new every listen. The perfect balance of energy and interest for sessions long enough that your mind needs company.

De La Soul — Stakes Is High. If you know 3 Feet High but not this one, the shift in tone will surprise you — more serious, more considered, still groovy. Good for a workout discovery listen when you want something with a bit more weight behind it.
How long is the journey?
branch.fork: Long — more than 45 minutes
Short — under 20 minutes
Very long — multiple hours, needs to sustain

branch.content: A mix that runs unattended, or something you already know?
branch.fork: A mix — set it and forget it
Something familiar

branch.content: What mood for the road?
branch.fork: Alert and energised
Smooth and flowing

branch.content: DnB or breaks?
branch.fork: DnB — fast and percussive
Breaks — more rhythmically varied

branch.content: Dancefloor energy or dark and technical?
branch.fork: Dancefloor — crowd energy in the car
Dark and technical — focused and serious

branch.content: DJ Hype FabricLive 03. Jump-up DnB built for rooms full of people. That crowd energy carries into a car — it is hard to feel tired or disengaged with this on. Relentlessly fun without being mindless.

Noisia FabricLive 40 or Ed Rush and Optical Virus Vaults. Both are serious enough to keep full attention engaged across a long drive without tipping into something oppressive. Pick Noisia if you want more variety, Virus Vaults for pure consistency.
UK sound or American breaks?
branch.fork: UK — big, physical, forward
Turntablist precision — technique as rhythm

branch.content: Freestylers or Stanton Warriors. Wide stereo image, punchy bass, enough variation to hold attention across a journey. UK breaks physicality works well at car volume — you feel the low end and the rhythm keeps you alert.

DJ Craze — Girls on the Floor remaster. Scratch technique and breaks in mix format. The precision is its own kind of alertness. Nothing else asks for your attention quite the same way — your brain follows the timing involuntarily.
Melodic and emotional or smooth and rhythmic?
branch.fork: Melodic and emotional
Smooth and rhythmic — groove without feeling

branch.content: Have you heard LTJ Bukem and Calibre before?
branch.fork: Yes, I know them
No — this is new territory

branch.content: Do you need to think, or do you need to stop thinking?
branch.fork: I need space to think
I need to stop thinking — occupy my mind

branch.content: LTJ Bukem FabricLive 46. Atmospheric DnB that opens space rather than filling it. Good for drives where you need your mind free to work on something else. The music creates a container for thought rather than competing with it.

Calibre FabricLive 68. Warm, musicianly liquid DnB that gives your mind enough to follow without demanding concentration. The emotional intelligence in his mixes is satisfying enough to occupy you completely without effort.

Start with Calibre FabricLive 68 — the most immediately accessible of the liquid mixes. Melodic enough to connect with on a first listen, deep enough to keep rewarding across a long drive. If it lands, go to Marcus Intalex FabricLive 35 next time.

DJ Marky FabricLive 55 or Aphrodite melodic mix. Brazilian DnB influence gives Marky's mix an irresistible forward groove — rhythmic without emotional weight. Aphrodite if you want something slightly warmer and more melodic.
Electronic or hip hop?
branch.fork: Electronic
Hip hop

branch.content: Cinematic and abstract or more structured?
branch.fork: Cinematic — I want it to score the journey
More structured — rhythm-driven but interesting

branch.content: Amon Tobin — Out From Out Where. Abstract, textural, shifts like a journey does. You will arrive somewhere mentally different to where you started. Makes a familiar road feel like new terrain.

Amon Tobin — Supermodified. More rhythm-driven than his later work — intricate but grounded. Good if you want his sonic world without the full abstraction. Also works for drives where you need to stay alert as well as interested.
Energy or comfort?
branch.fork: Energy — keep me sharp
Comfort — warm, familiar, no surprises

branch.content: Beastie Boys — Paul's Boutique. Relentless sample density and energy. An album that rewards familiarity — you catch something new every time. Hard to feel tired behind the wheel with this on.
De La Soul — Stakes Is High or 3 Feet High. Warm, witty, no rough edges. Good company for a road you know well. Nothing demands your attention but everything rewards it if you offer it.
Does the music need to do something, or just fill the time?
branch.fork: Set a mood — I need to arrive in a specific state
Just fill it pleasantly

branch.content: Pump up or bring down?
branch.fork: Pump me up — I need to arrive ready
Calm me down — I need to arrive composed

branch.content: DJ Craze or Deekline and Ed Solo. Short, punchy, full of attitude. The Deekline Booty Breaks Vol 1 intro is one of the best arriving-ready moments in breaks. You will get out of the car already where you need to be.

Something from the Seba or Calibre end of things. Rhythmic enough to give you structure, calm enough to bring your temperature down. The half-step roll is almost meditative at low-medium volume in a car.
Something you know or something random?
branch.fork: Something I know well
Surprise me

branch.content: Pick a single track you love and let it carry the drive. One great song beats three average ones. Short drives with the right track are often better than long drives with the wrong album.

Put a FabricLive mix on and listen to whatever track comes up. Twenty minutes is enough to find at least one thing you want to hear again. Treat it as a discovery exercise on a route you already know.
Does the music need to sustain across everything, or will you change it in phases?
branch.fork: One thing that lasts the whole drive
I will manage it in phases

branch.content: Narrative and evolving, or consistent and reliable?
branch.fork: Narrative — I want it to shift as the drive does
Consistent — same energy start to finish

branch.content: Amon Tobin — Out From Out Where into Supermodified back to back. Three-plus hours that shift in texture and intensity across the journey. Treat it as a scored drive rather than background. You will arrive feeling like you went somewhere.

Stack FabricLive 35, 44, and 68 together — Marcus Intalex, Commix, and Calibre. About three hours of consistent, warm, musicianly DnB. Reliable across any long drive, similar enough in energy to feel unbroken.

Plan it in stages. Something mellow to settle in — LTJ Bukem or Calibre. More energy as you hit your stride — DJ Hype or Noisia. Something to arrive with — DJ Craze or Deekline for the final stretch. The music marks the journey's progress.
What kind of social situation?
branch.fork: Two or three people — intimate, everyone knows each other
Small group — five to ten, varied familiarity
Bigger gathering — proper party or event

branch.content: Are you all into the same music, or is it a mix?
branch.fork: All on the same page — scene people
Not all into electronic — need common ground

branch.content: What is the music doing in the room?
branch.fork: Actively listening and discussing it
Background while doing something else
Dancing, even in a small space

branch.content: Show them something new or enjoy something you all know?
branch.fork: Show them something — shared discovery
Something we all know — enjoy it together

branch.content: How adventurous is the group?
branch.fork: Very — take us somewhere genuinely unfamiliar
Adventurous but still within the scene

branch.content: The Bug — Fire or London Zoo. Even for scene people, this is probably outside the comfort zone — the dancehall and industrial crossover is genuinely unlike anything in DnB or breaks. Worth a group listen precisely because it will start an argument.

Cujo — Adventures in Foam. Shadow Records-era DnB that sounds nothing like what you expect from the label. Experimental, atmospheric, ahead of its time. Great for a small group that knows the scene but has not heard everything in it.

Amon Tobin — Supermodified or Out From Out Where. If you all know his work it becomes a conversation about which album is better. If one person does not know it yet, watching them hear it for the first time is worth the evening.
High energy or low and comfortable?
branch.fork: High — keep the energy up
Low — relaxed evening background

branch.content: Deekline and Ed Solo or DJ Craze. High-energy breaks in a small space — not so demanding it takes over the evening, punchy enough to keep the energy right. Both have enough character that they reward scene knowledge without requiring it.
Seba or Marcus Intalex FabricLive 35. Warm, rolling DnB that sounds good at low volume without losing its quality. The kind of choice that makes an evening feel well-curated rather than well-planned.
Proper sound system or small setup?
branch.fork: Proper speakers — room to move and feel it
Small setup — kitchen or living room dancing

branch.content: Build it like a proper set. Open groove-heavy — DJ Marky or Aphrodite. Build toward the heavier material — Noisia or Ed Rush when the room is ready. Close with DJ Craze for pure technique or Calibre for emotional landing. Earn the heavy stuff first.

UK Garage or Bassline. DJ Q FabricLive 99 or the Speed Garage mix. The tempo is exactly right for a small space — enough to move to, not so demanding people feel self-conscious. The groove is infectious without being confrontational.
Is there a shared genre to anchor on, or truly mixed?
branch.fork: Hip hop as common ground — most people have some connection
Genuinely mixed — no clear shared reference

branch.content: Accessible immediately or intelligent and rewarding?
branch.fork: Accessible — I want everyone to enjoy it straight away
Intelligent — something that rewards attention

branch.content: Beastie Boys Licensed to Ill or De La Soul 3 Feet High and Rising. Licensed to Ill if you want immediate physical energy — it hits everyone the same way. 3 Feet High if you want something with more warmth and wit. Both are safe bets in mixed company.

De La Soul Stakes Is High or Beastie Boys Paul's Boutique. Both reward close listening without demanding it. Stakes Is High ages better the more hip hop you know. Paul's Boutique is the more immediately surprising — the sample density catches people who are not looking for it.
Introduce them to your world or stay neutral?
branch.fork: Introduce them — use the opportunity
Stay neutral — keep everyone comfortable

branch.content: DJ Aphrodite melodic mix. The best gateway into DnB for people who do not know the scene — melodic, non-aggressive, immediately enjoyable. If this does not land with someone, nothing in the genre will. Have the story of the music ready to tell alongside it.

Clams Casino instrumentals. The most universally palatable thing in this collection — beat music that sounds contemporary, interesting without being challenging, and sits under conversation without dominating it. Works every time with every kind of person.
Is the group there for the music, or is it background to conversation?
branch.fork: There for the music — it matters
Background — conversation is the point

branch.content: How much does the group know?
branch.fork: Mostly scene — they will recognise a lot
Mixed — some know it, some do not

branch.content: Play the acknowledged classics or take some risks?
branch.fork: The classics — give them what they came for
Take risks — things they probably have not heard

branch.content: FabricLive 03, 40, and 46 rotating. Covers the main energy zones — jump-up, neurofunk, and atmospheric. Everyone will know at least one and feel the recognition. The contrast between them becomes the conversation.

Blunted on Shadow Records or kyo006 Futurism. Records from credible corners of the scene that rarely get played. Rewarding for people who know the reference points but have not heard everything. Invites conversation rather than closing it.
Lean toward the knowledgeable listeners or bring the others in?
branch.fork: Reward the people who know the scene
Bring the others in first

branch.content: Open with Aphrodite or DJ Marky to establish the context and warm the room. Then move to the material the scene people will respond to. The newcomers will follow if the energy builds naturally rather than arriving fully formed.

Aphrodite melodic or DJ Marky FabricLive 55. Genuine about the genre while being immediately accessible. Scene people will respect the choice even while finding it familiar. Gets everyone in the same room before you go further.
What energy level suits the gathering?
branch.fork: High — keep the room lively
Medium — relaxed but present
Low — evening winding down

branch.content: Freestylers, Stanton Warriors, or Deekline and Ed Solo in rotation. UK breaks at gathering volume — engaging without being imposing. Lively enough to keep energy up, familiar enough that nobody feels excluded.
Clams Casino instrumentals or Luomo. Both hold up at conversation volume without losing their character. Interesting enough to mention if someone asks, easy enough to talk over if they do not.

Four Tet FabricLive 59 or Amon Tobin at low volume. The room is settling, conversations are getting smaller and more personal. Music that fits that shift — present, not demanding, changes the texture of the space without taking it over.
Are you playing the music or listening to someone else?
branch.fork: I am playing it
Someone else is — I am just there

branch.content: What does the crowd know and expect?
branch.fork: Scene people — they came for this specifically
Mixed — you need to read the room

branch.content: What stage of the night is it?
branch.fork: Early — warming up
Peak — the room is going
Winding down — close it properly

branch.content: Aphrodite or DJ Marky. Melodic DnB that invites people in without demanding they commit. The right choice for a room still finding its feet — builds trust before it builds energy.

DJ Hype for jump-up energy, Noisia or Ed Rush and Optical for the technical end. Read which way the room is leaning — both work but they reward different things in a crowd. If you are unsure, Hype is safer at peak.

Calibre or LTJ Bukem. Bring the energy down gracefully — atmospheric DnB that keeps the rhythm but releases the physical intensity. The room will follow the music down if you do it gradually rather than suddenly.

Aphrodite melodic or Freestylers. The safest credible choices for a mixed crowd at a big gathering. Accessible but not embarrassing — you will not regret either, and most people will respond without needing to be told why.
Music you know or something you are discovering in a crowd?
branch.fork: I know it — I am in familiar territory
I do not know it — discovering it live

branch.content: Let yourself respond to the room rather than the record. Familiar music in a good crowd hits differently — the collective experience adds something that listening alone cannot. Trust what you know about the track and let the room give it new meaning.

One of the best ways to encounter music — hearing it for the first time while it is working on a room. Pay attention to what the crowd responds to. Ask someone what the track is. The memory of the moment becomes part of the record permanently.
What time of day is it?
branch.fork: Morning — starting the day
Afternoon — middle of the day
Evening
Late night — the small hours

branch.content: How awake are you?
branch.fork: Not quite — ease me in
Ready — I want energy straight away

branch.content: Rhythm or near-ambient?
branch.fork: Some rhythm — a gentle beat to wake into
Near-ambient — I am not ready for beats yet

branch.content: Luomo at low volume or early LTJ Bukem. Both have enough rhythm to keep you present without demanding anything. Good company for a slow morning without being intrusive or energetic enough to feel wrong for the hour.

Clams Casino instrumentals played quietly. The hazy, cloud-rap texture is genuinely ambient at low volume. Something to wake up into gradually rather than wake up to abruptly. Let the room fill with it before you decide what you actually want.
Physical energy or mental sharpness?
branch.fork: Physical — get the body going before the mind
Mental — sharpen up, start thinking

branch.content: DJ Aphrodite or Freestylers. Upbeat and immediate — the kind of music that makes you move before you have decided to. Good for mornings when you need momentum before motivation arrives.

De La Soul — Stakes Is High. Sharp, considered, lyrically intelligent. Hip hop that makes you think without demanding you sit down and concentrate. Good for mornings when your head needs to engage rather than your body.
Actively listening or background for something else?
branch.fork: Actively listening — this is the activity
Background — doing something else alongside it

branch.content: What are you looking for from the listen?
branch.fork: Technical appreciation — study the craft
Emotion — I want to feel something
Discovery — something I have not heard
Nostalgia — something I know and love

branch.content: Production craft or compositional structure?
branch.fork: Production — how sounds are made and shaped
Composition — how a piece is built and developed

branch.content: Noisia FabricLive 40 in good headphones. The compression, layering, bass design — a production masterclass. Listen to how each element sits in the mix. There is nothing wasted, nothing accidental. Takes multiple listens to hear how much work went into it.

Amon Tobin — Out From Out Where. Every track is a structural puzzle. Sample sources unrecognisable, architecture deliberate and intricate. Try to map what you hear to the choices being made. You will not fully succeed, which is the point.
What kind of emotion?
branch.fork: Melancholy — searching, open
Euphoric — I want to feel the highs
Introspective — quiet and inward

branch.content: Calibre. His music sits permanently in the space between sadness and resolution — never fully dark, never falsely hopeful. FabricLive 68 is the best entry point. Play it with your eyes closed. There is real intelligence in how it manages feeling without sentimentality.

DJ Hype FabricLive 03 or DJ Marky FabricLive 55. Jump-up DnB and Brazilian liquid at their most unambiguously joyful. The highs of DnB require music built specifically to deliver them — this is that music. Loud. No apologising for it.

LTJ Bukem — Logical Progression or FabricLive 46. Atmospheric DnB that opens space for thought rather than filling it. Creates a container rather than a content. Good for afternoons when you need to be with something without being asked to react to it.
Deeper within what you know, or genuinely outside it?
branch.fork: Within what I know — deeper cuts, earlier records
Something genuinely different

branch.content: Which corner has the most unexplored depth?
branch.fork: DnB — I have not heard all of it
Hip hop — golden era gaps I have not filled

branch.content: Cujo — Adventures in Foam (1997 Shadow reissue) or Blunted on Shadow Records. Both are Shadow Records-era records from before the label defined its identity. Experimental, forward-looking, and largely unheard compared to the main catalogue. This is the DNA of a lot of what came after.

Give Em Enough Dope Volume 1 or the ATM Magazine compilations. Deep cuts from the golden era that never got the attention they deserved. Rich territory if you know De La Soul and Beastie Boys but not what surrounded them in the same conversations.
How far outside?
branch.fork: Adjacent — similar grammar, different accent
Genuinely outside — I want to be challenged

branch.content: Luomo — Vocalcity. Finnish minimal house that shares the rhythmic intelligence of DnB but sounds completely different. The same obsessive attention to groove and texture, an entirely different sonic world. What DnB producers listen to when they want perspective.

The Bug — London Zoo. Industrial, dancehall, grime, and DnB in a sound that belongs to none of them. Abrasive, loud, confrontational. Give it two full listens before deciding — it takes that long to understand what it is actually doing.
Which era?
branch.fork: Early to mid 90s — the formative years
Late 90s to early 2000s — the golden era
Mid 2000s — the FabricLive era

branch.content: Blunted on Shadow Records or the 1997 Cujo Adventures In Foam reissue. Shadow Records before it was an institution — DnB and hip hop informing each other before either had fully defined itself. The energy of a scene that had not yet decided what it was.

Beastie Boys — Paul's Boutique for hip hop, Ed Rush and Optical — Wormhole for DnB. Both from the point at which their genres were fully formed and at their most confident. Revisit them as the records they actually are rather than background.

FabricLive 03, 35, 40, or 46. These mixes documented a period when DnB was being taken seriously as music rather than just as a club format. Revisiting them now has historical weight they did not carry at the time.
Energy or neutral?
branch.fork: Energy — keep the afternoon moving
Neutral — fill the space without intruding

branch.content: DJ Aphrodite 2010 mix or DJ Marky FabricLive 55. Melodic DnB that provides energy without pulling focus. The kind of music you notice in a good way every few minutes, then forget again. Keeps the temperature right across a long afternoon session.
Luomo at low volume or Clams Casino instrumentals. Both function as ambient without losing identity. Make a room feel inhabited and considered without asking you to pay attention to them.
Winding down or still active?
branch.fork: Winding down — the day is finishing
Still active — energy remaining

branch.content: Decompress or close the day with something considered?
branch.fork: Decompress — shed the day
Close well — mark the end deliberately

branch.content: How much tension are you carrying?
branch.fork: A lot — match it first, then bring it down
Some — gentle decompression is enough

branch.content: Start with Ed Rush and Optical or Noisia and let it burn off what you are carrying. Then transition to Calibre or Seba as things settle. Match the tension first — do not start mellow when you are not mellow. The music cannot take you somewhere you have not left yet.
Seba mixes or FabricLive 50 Autonomic. The half-step roll is decompression in rhythm form — still moving forward, but without urgency. Exactly the right pace for winding down without forcing it.
Something complete in itself or open-ended?
branch.fork: A proper record with a shape — beginning, middle, end
A mix that can keep going as long as needed

branch.content: Amon Tobin — Out From Out Where or De La Soul — Stakes Is High. Both work as complete statements rather than background. Albums designed to be heard start to finish. A satisfying way to close a day when you want something with a sense of shape.

Calibre FabricLive 68 or Marcus Intalex FabricLive 35. Mix format means no obligation to stop at a particular point. Let them run as long as the evening does. The quality holds regardless of where you come in or go out.
Getting ready to go out or in for the night with energy left?
branch.fork: Getting ready to go out
In for the night but still buzzing

branch.content: How long until you leave?
branch.fork: An hour or more — time to build properly
Nearly ready — need the energy now

branch.content: Start with Aphrodite melodic or DJ Marky. After 30 minutes move to Deekline and Ed Solo or Freestylers. Final stretch: DJ Hype or DJ Craze. Arrive already in the room mentally. The build is as important as the destination.
DJ Craze or Noisia straight in. No build needed — you want to arrive already at the level. Something that hits immediately and holds there for the 15 minutes you have left before the door.
Sustain the energy or let it settle naturally?
branch.fork: Sustain it — not ready to wind down
Let it settle — riding the tail end

branch.content: Breaks or DnB at full volume. Freestylers, DJ Craze, or Deekline — anything with consistent forward energy. Match the level you are at. Do not let the music pull you down before you are ready to go there.
Seba or Marcus Intalex. Ride the energy down without cutting it. The half-step roll lets your tempo slow naturally alongside the music. You will not notice when you stopped moving with it.
Alone or with someone?
branch.fork: Alone — completely
With someone, but the night is quiet

branch.content: What is the late-night headspace?
branch.fork: Introspective — things to sit with
Restless — too much energy, need to spend it
Floating — I want to disappear

branch.content: Heavy or gentle?
branch.fork: Heavy — something with weight and friction
Gentle — hold the space without pressure

branch.content: The Bug — Fire. Low frequencies, slow tempo, physical weight. Does not try to fix anything — just sits in the dark with you. Loud headphones, lights off. One of the few records that genuinely feels like company in a difficult moment.

Clams Casino instrumentals. The haze and warmth of the production is exactly right for late-night introspection that does not need to be heavy. Sits alongside thoughts without amplifying them. Oddly comforting in the small hours.
Spend the energy or let it run?
branch.fork: Spend it — channel it somewhere
Let it run — I do not mind being wired

branch.content: Put on Amon Tobin or Noisia in headphones and let the mental energy go into the music. Redirect the restlessness into listening. By the time the record finishes you will be ready to sleep. Technical complexity absorbs restlessness more efficiently than anything else.

DJ Hype or DJ Craze loud in headphones. If you are wired anyway, commit to it. Move around, let it go. Better than fighting the energy at midnight. You will crash more completely on the other side of it.

LTJ Bukem — Logical Progression or FabricLive 50 Autonomic. Both built to dissolve the room around you. Atmospheric DnB at its most expansive. Eyes closed, headphones on. Let the space open up. The music will carry you if you let it.
What does the space feel like?
branch.fork: Comfortable and close — good company, soft energy
The night is nearly done

branch.content: Four Tet FabricLive 59 or Seba mixes. Warm, considered electronic music that suits close company at low volume without demanding attention from either of you. Sounds like a good choice rather than a default.

Luomo quietly or Clams Casino instrumentals barely audible. Music that will still be playing when the conversation has stopped. Something to fall asleep to without noticing the transition. Let it run — there is no wrong place to stop.
What kind of work?
branch.fork: Creative — writing, designing, making something
Analytical — thinking, problem-solving, reading
Repetitive or mechanical — grinding through tasks
Collaborative — calls or working near other people

branch.content: Do lyrics pull you out of the work?
branch.fork: Yes — they compete with my own thinking
No — I can filter them out

branch.content: What does the work need from the music?
branch.fork: A sonic environment — music as atmosphere
Rhythm and momentum — music as engine
Separation from the world — music as barrier

branch.content: What kind of creative work?
branch.fork: Visual — design, illustration, art
Written — copy, articles, longer prose
Making music or audio

branch.content: Amon Tobin — Out From Out Where. The abstraction suits visual thinking in a way more linear music does not. The sounds suggest spaces rather than narratives. Genuinely useful for visual work, not just pleasant — the brain processes it alongside rather than instead of the work.

Clams Casino instrumentals. Vocal-textured without actual words — the brain hears something language-adjacent without processing language. Paradoxically useful for writing. Hazy, warm, and somehow unobtrusive for something so distinctive.

LTJ Bukem FabricLive 46 or Logical Progression. Clear structural logic in the mix — the way Bukem builds and releases tension is a lesson in arrangement. Useful to have in your ears while making decisions about your own music. You absorb the structure without being consciously aware of it.
Short session or long?
branch.fork: Short — under an hour
Long — two hours or more

branch.content: Seba 70 minute mix. Just long enough for a focused burst without needing to make another decision mid-session. Press play at the start, notice when it ends — the session lives inside the music rather than the other way around.

Marcus Intalex FabricLive 35 and Calibre FabricLive 68 back to back. Similar energy, slightly different textures. The transition between the two refreshes without interrupting. About three hours of consistent, warm DnB — reliable across any long session without requiring you to make a decision mid-flow.
Internal distraction or external noise?
branch.fork: Internal — I need to stop thinking about other things
External — I need to block out my environment

branch.content: Noisia FabricLive 40 at a level that demands some attention. The production is complex enough to occupy the background processing of your brain without pulling conscious focus from the work. Keeps the anxious part busy so the working part can function.

Ed Rush and Optical — Virus Vaults Mix at volume. Dense, complex, and loud enough in headphones to completely eliminate environmental noise. The music fills every available cognitive space. Nothing gets through. The bluntness of the approach is the point.
Lift or sustain?
branch.fork: Lift — I need energy to get into it
Sustain — I am in it, I just need to stay there

branch.content: De La Soul or Beastie Boys. Energising without aggression. The lyrical density gives something to half-process while the groove does the lifting. Good for work that needs a running start rather than a steady state.

DJ Aphrodite melodic or FabricLive 68 Calibre at comfortable volume. Familiar enough not to distract, present enough to prevent drifting. Sustains a working state without pulling you in or out of it. The difference between good company and demanding company.
How much cognitive space does the work leave?
branch.fork: Not much — demanding, minimal distraction needed
Some — music can be present without interfering

branch.content: Near silence or just enough variation to stay out of the way?
branch.fork: As minimal as possible — close to silence
Some variation, nothing that demands attention

branch.content: Luomo — Vocalcity at low volume. Minimal house so restrained it functions almost as textured silence. Fills the cognitive gap without creating one of its own. The right choice when the work needs everything you have and music is just keeping the silence from being oppressive.

FabricLive 50 Autonomic. Half-step DnB stripped to structural essentials. Enough variation to stay interesting if you notice it, controlled enough to disappear completely if you do not. The ideal balance for demanding analytical work.
Energy behind the thinking or space for the thinking?
branch.fork: Energy — propel the thinking forward
Space — music that creates room rather than filling it

branch.content: DJ Marky FabricLive 55 or Deekline and Ed Solo. Groove-forward music that keeps energy up without demanding mental engagement. The rhythm does the work so your thinking can.

LTJ Bukem — Logical Progression. Atmospheric DnB that opens space rather than fills it. Some music is good to think in. This is that music. Nothing else in this collection does it quite as consistently.
How long is the session?
branch.fork: Short — under an hour, just push through
Long — needs to actively help you sustain

branch.content: Aggression or groove?
branch.fork: Aggression — make it feel urgent
Groove — make it feel effortless

branch.content: Freeparty Filth or Noisia. Put the clock away, put the music on, use the aggression as momentum. The work finishes when the track does — or at least that is the useful illusion.
Deekline and Ed Solo or DJ Aphrodite. Groove-driven enough that you stop noticing what you are doing and start noticing the music instead. The work happens in the space between.
Variety or consistency?
branch.fork: Variety — I lose focus if nothing changes
Consistency — same energy is an anchor

branch.content: Stack mixes with different energies and change them every hour. DJ Marky for warmth, Freestylers for mid-session energy, something from the FabricLive series for the final stretch. The changes mark the time and reset focus without requiring you to stop.

Aphrodite 1 Hour Mix or the Overdrive mix. Consistent pace across a long runtime. One decision made once, nothing required of you after that. The music is the constant while everything else is variable.
Can others hear the music?
branch.fork: Yes — needs to work for the room
No — headphones, only you hear it

branch.content: Professional or relaxed environment?
branch.fork: Professional — needs to be inoffensive background
Relaxed — people will notice and respond

branch.content: Luomo or Clams Casino instrumentals at low volume. Both are interesting enough to elevate a space without being provocative. Nobody will object and at least one person will probably ask what it is by the end of the day.

De La Soul or Beastie Boys. Familiar enough that people recognise it as music rather than noise, interesting enough to invite comment. Opens more conversations than it closes. Low-risk choice that still says something about taste.

Use the isolation. Put on something you would not play out loud — Noisia FabricLive 40 or Ed Rush and Optical at a volume that would be antisocial in the room. Headphone volume is a private luxury. Make use of it.
What is your starting point?
branch.fork: A genre I know well — I want to go deeper into it
Adjacent to what I know — similar thinking, different world
Something completely outside my usual listening

branch.content: Which corner has the most unexplored depth?
branch.fork: DnB — I know the surface, not the roots
Breaks — I know the big names, not what surrounds them
Hip hop — golden era gaps I have not filled

branch.content: Which direction within DnB interests you?
branch.fork: The early roots — where did all this come from?
The experimental edges — what happened at the margins?
The musical end — DnB made by musicians for musicians

branch.content: Blunted on Shadow Records and the 1997 Cujo Adventures In Foam. Shadow before it was an institution — experimental, boundary-free, equally informed by hip hop, ambient, and jazz. This is the DNA of much of what followed. Listen to it and then listen to Amon Tobin and hear where it led.

FabricLive 50 Autonomic and kyo006 Futurism. Both represent DnB taking itself apart to ask what was actually necessary. Minimal, strange, and still not fully absorbed by the mainstream. Worth the time if you want something that has not yet been fully processed by the scene.

Calibre FabricLive 68, then Marcus Intalex FabricLive 35, then Commix FabricLive 44. Three perspectives on liquid DnB from producers who approach it as composers. Start with Calibre — the most immediately musical. Give each a full uninterrupted listen before moving to the next.
More groove-oriented or more technically driven?
branch.fork: Groove — the swing and feel draws me
Technical — the craft and precision is what I respond to

branch.content: Tittsworth or Y4K DJ Icey. American breaks with deep hip hop roots — different from the UK sound. More swagger, more funk in the groove. Expands what you think breaks can be without requiring you to leave familiar rhythmic territory.

9B Deekline and Ed Solo or the ATM Magazine compilations. Technical breakbeat from the UK scene — precise, complex, and demanding enough to reward close listening. The kind of music that gives back more detail each time you return to it.
Deeper into artists you know, or the context around them?
branch.fork: Deeper — back catalogues and B-sides
Context — the scene and what surrounded them

branch.content: De La Soul have a deeper catalogue than most people realise. Buhloone Mindstate and De La Soul Is Dead are both essential and both very different from 3 Feet High. Go in order and pay attention to the deliberate shifts in tone between each record. The changes are the story.

Give Em Enough Dope Volume 1 and the ATM Magazine compilations. The context for golden era hip hop — records that were in the same conversations as De La Soul and the Beastie Boys but never crossed over as far. Rich, largely unheard territory with a clear connection to what you already know.
What is your strongest reference point to start from?
branch.fork: DnB — what else thinks the same way?
Breaks — what shares the groove but sounds different?
Hip hop — what else has the same rhythm intelligence?

branch.content: Which quality of DnB draws you most?
branch.fork: The rhythm — that bass and drum relationship
The atmosphere — how it opens or closes space
The production precision — the sound design craft

branch.content: UK Garage and Speed Garage. The half-time relationship between bass and drums in UKG is the closest rhythmic relative to DnB outside the genre itself. DJ Q FabricLive 99 is the best single entry point. You will hear the connection immediately and it will change how you hear both.

Four Tet FabricLive 59. Electronic music that uses space the way atmospheric DnB does, from a completely different starting point. The way he builds and holds tension is recognisably related even though the BPM and genre are entirely different.

Luomo — Vocalcity. Finnish minimal house with the same obsessive attention to rhythmic detail that good DnB has. The production intelligence is closely related even though everything else is different. This is what DnB producers listen to when they need perspective on their own work.
What aspect of breaks do you want to follow into new territory?
branch.fork: The physicality — something equally visceral
The sample culture and playfulness

branch.content: The Bug. If what you love about breaks is its physical directness and refusal to be polished, The Bug takes that quality somewhere else entirely. Heavy, confrontational, completely committed to its own logic. Not comfortable, not meant to be.

De La Soul or the Beastie Boys from the hip hop side. Sample culture in hip hop is the root of what breaks built on. Going back to 3 Feet High and Rising is hearing the grammar being invented before it became conventional. It explains a lot about why breaks sounds the way it does.
Which quality do you want to follow?
branch.fork: The verbal intelligence and lyricism
The groove and musicality
The production approach — sample construction and layering

branch.content: The deeper De La Soul catalogue — De La Soul Is Dead and Buhloone Mindstate. Lyrically their most demanding work. Then into the compilations around them for the broader conversation the music was part of.

DJ Marky FabricLive 55 or the Brazilian funk influence in melodic DnB. The groove in liquid DnB and the groove in hip hop share more than most people notice. Following Marky's Brazilian connection makes the link audible in a way that is hard to unhear.

Amon Tobin. The relationship between his sample-based construction and hip hop sample culture is direct even though the output is unrecognisably different. Paul's Boutique into Supermodified in one sitting is hearing what happens when the same impulse goes in two completely different directions.
How far outside do you want to go?
branch.fork: Significantly outside but still approachable
As far as possible — I want to be genuinely unsettled

branch.content: The Bug — London Zoo as a starting point. Connects to enough things you already know — the bass weight of DnB, the rhythmic sensibility of breaks, the vocal tradition of reggae and dancehall — that it is not incomprehensible. But it sounds like nothing else in your collection.

The Bug — Fire. The most extreme and confrontational record in a related orbit. Industrial, dancehall, and DnB stripped of anything that might make it comfortable. Your complete attention, good headphones. It will either do nothing for you or change something permanently. There is no middle ground with this record.
